prerequisites: A knowledge of the specification for P1 and its associated formulae is assumed and may be tested.
unitDescription: Proof; algebra and functions; coordinate geometry in the (x, y) plane; sequences and series; exponentials and logarithms; trigonometry; differentiation; integration.

paperFormat.formulaBooklet: Mathematical Formulae and Statistical Tables provided

sections.title: Structure of mathematical proof
sections.content: Understand and use the structure of mathematical proof, proceeding from given assumptions through a series of logical steps to a conclusion; use methods of proof stated below.

sections.title: Proof by exhaustion
sections.guidance: This involves trying all the options. Suppose x and y are odd integers less than 7. Prove that their sum is divisible by 2.

sections.title: Disproof by counter-example
sections.guidance: Disproof by counter example — show that the statement '$n^2 - n + 1$ is a prime number for all values of $n$' is untrue.

sections.title: Algebraic division, factor and remainder theorems
sections.title_zh: 代数除法、因式定理与余式定理
sections.content: Simple algebraic division; use of the Factor Theorem and the Remainder Theorem.
sections.guidance: Only division by $(ax + b)$ or $(ax - b)$ will be required. Students should know that if $f(x) = 0$ when $x = \frac{b}{a}$, then $(ax - b)$ is a factor of $f(x)$. Students may be required to factorise cubic expressions such as $x^3 + 3x^2 - 4$ and $6x^3 + 11x^2 - x - 6$. Students should be familiar with the terms 'quotient' and 'remainder' and be able to determine the remainder when the polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(ax + b)$.

chapters.title: Coordinate Geometry in the (x, y) Plane

sections.title: Equation of a circle
sections.content: Coordinate geometry of the circle using the equation of a circle in the form $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ and including use of the following circle properties: (i) the angle in a semicircle is a right angle; (ii) the perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord; (iii) the perpendicularity of radius and tangent.
sections.guidance: Students should be able to find the radius and the coordinates of the centre of the circle, given the equation of the circle and vice versa.

sections.title: Sequences and recurrence relations
sections.content: Sequences, including those given by a formula for the $n$th term and those generated by a simple relation of the form $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$.

sections.title: Arithmetic sequences and series
sections.content: Understand and work with arithmetic sequences and series, including the formula for the $n$th term and the sum of a finite arithmetic series; the sum of the first $n$ natural numbers. Understanding of $\sum$ notation will be expected.
sections.guidance: The proof of the sum formula should be known.

sections.title: Increasing, decreasing and periodic sequences
sections.content: Increasing sequences, decreasing sequences and periodic sequences.

sections.title: Geometric sequences and series
sections.content: Understand and work with geometric sequences and series, including the formulae for the $n$th term and the sum of a finite geometric series; the sum to infinity of a convergent geometric series, including the use of $|r| < 1$.
sections.guidance: Given the sum of a series students should be able to use logs to find the value of $n$. The proof of the sum formula for a finite series should be known. The sum to infinity may be expressed as $S_\infty$.

sections.title: Binomial expansion
sections.content: Binomial expansion of $(a + bx)^n$ for positive integer $n$.
sections.guidance: The notations $n!$, $\binom{n}{r}$ and $^nC_r$ may be used.

sections.title: Laws of logarithms
sections.content: Laws of logarithms: $\log_a(xy) \equiv \log_a x + \log_a y$, $\log_a\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) \equiv \log_a x - \log_a y$, $\log_a(x^k) \equiv k\log_a x$, $\log_a\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \equiv -\log_a x$, $\log_a a = 1$, where $a, x, y > 0$, $a \neq 1$.

sections.title: Solving exponential equations
sections.content: The solution of equations of the form $a^x = b$.
sections.guidance: Students may use the change of base formula.

sections.title: Solving trigonometric equations
sections.content: Solution of simple trigonometric equations in a given interval.
sections.guidance: Students should be able to solve equations such as $\sin\left(x + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{3}{4}$ for $0 < x < 2\pi$, $\cos(x + 30°) = \frac{1}{2}$ for $-180° < x < 180°$, $\tan 2x = 1$ for $90° < x < 270°$, $6\cos^2 x + \sin x - 5 = 0$ for $0 \leq x < 360°$, $\sin^2\left(x + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$ for $-\pi \leq x < \pi$.

sections.title: Maxima, minima and stationary points
sections.content: Applications of differentiation to maxima and minima and stationary points, increasing and decreasing functions.
sections.guidance: To include applications to curve sketching. Maxima and minima problems may be set in the context of a practical problem.

sections.title: Area under a curve
sections.content: Interpretation of the definite integral as the area under a curve.
sections.guidance: Students will be expected to be able to evaluate the area of a region bounded by a curve and given straight lines. For example, find the finite area bounded by the curve $y = 6x - x^2$ and the line $y = 2x$. $\int x\,\mathrm{d}y$ will not be required. Students will be expected to be able to evaluate the area of a region bounded by two curves.

sections.title: The trapezium rule
sections.content: Approximation of area under a curve using the trapezium rule.
sections.guidance: For example, use the trapezium rule to approximate $\int_0^1 \sqrt{2x+1}\,\mathrm{d}x$ using four strips. Use of increasing number of trapezia to improve accuracy and an estimate of the error may be required.

AO1.detail: Students should be able to: select and correctly carry out routine procedures; accurately recall facts, terminology and definitions.

AO2.detail: Students should be able to: construct rigorous mathematical arguments; make deductions and inferences; assess the validity of mathematical arguments; explain their reasoning; use mathematical language and notation correctly.

AO3.description: Solve problems within mathematics and in other contexts
AO3.detail: Students should be able to: translate problems in mathematical and non-mathematical contexts into mathematical processes; interpret solutions to problems in their original context; evaluate methods used and results obtained.
